3A_Bibliographic.citation: Must, A., Eliasziw, M., Stanish, H., Curtin, C., Bandini, L. G., & Bowling, A. (2023). Passive and social screen time in children with autism and in association with obesity. Frontiers in Pediatrics, 11, 1198033.

3B_Sample_Data.abcd_release: Not explicitly stated; baseline data collected September 2016 - October 2018
3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: A total of 10,842 children from the ABCD study were included in the analyses, of which 187 (1.7%) were reported to have been diagnosed with autism. Propensity score matching yielded a data set consisting of 186 children with autism and 930 typically developing children.
3B_Sample_Data.notes: Propensity score matched sample: 186 autism + 930 TD = 1,116 total.

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Screen time duration by type (passive viewing, video games, social/video chat, texting) and by autism status and gender
3C_Exposure.modalities: passive viewing (TV, movies, videos), video games, social media/video chat, texting
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Continuous (mean hours or minutes per day); also categorical for obesity analysis (<2h, 2-3.99h, 4+h for passive; <30min, 30-59min, 60+min for games; 0min, 1-44min, 45+min for social; 0min, 1-29min, 30+min for texting)
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Screen time was measured using the Youth Screen Time Survey. Children answered questions about typical hours per day spent on six different screen activities, separately for weekdays and weekend days. The 7-point scale responses (none, <30 min, 30 min, 1 h, 2 h, 3 h, and 4+ hours) were converted to corresponding count measures. For each category, daily screen time was calculated from the weighted average of the weekday and weekend screen time activities.

3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: BMI calculated from measured height and weight, classified using CDC 2001 age- and sex-specific z-scores
3D_Outcome.base_rate_if_binary: 18.8% among children with autism, 14.4% among typically developing children
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: BMI was calculated as weight in kg divided by height in meters squared. Sex and age-specific BMI z-scores were referenced against the Center of Disease Control 2001. BMI z-scores and classified as obese (>=95th percentile) or not (<95th percentile).

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Generalized estimation equations (GEE) with binomial distribution, log link (for prevalence ratios), and Poisson distribution (for screen time duration); propensity score matched design
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: None beyond propensity score matching (matched on age, gender, race/ethnicity, socioeconomic position)
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: A propensity score analysis was used to create a matched data set for the analyses. Generalized linear models using generalized estimation equations were used to derive the estimates and inferences. For duration of screen time analyses, the data were modelled using a Poisson distribution. For analyzing obesity, generalized linear models using generalized estimation equations were used with a binomial distribution with a log link. A compound symmetry correlation structure was used to account for the within-site clustering of observations.
3E_Design_Analysis.notes: No covariates needed in analysis because propensity score matching balanced groups.

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Passive screen time 4+ hours/day (vs. <2h) - all children
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: 4 h or more: prevalence ratio 2.15 (1.40-3.31), p<0.001

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Video games 60+ min/day (vs. <30 min) - all children
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: 60 min or more: prevalence ratio 1.58 (1.09-2.29), p=0.01
30 min or more: prevalence ratio 1.42 (1.00-2.01), p=0.05
Boys - Watch TV: Autism 2.9, Typical 2.3, Ratio 1.26 (1.13-1.42), p<0.001

3G_Reporting_Quality.abstract_significance_verbatim: Significant mean differences were observed by autism status and gender for both passive screen use and playing video games.
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: Paper does not discuss effect size magnitude. Associations between screen time and obesity did not differ by autism status (non-significant interactions).

3H_Recommendations.notes: The paper notes that highly prescriptive guidelines have been replaced by a more balanced approach emphasizing quality over quantity. It does not issue direct recommendations to limit screen time.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion takes a notably balanced approach. It describes differences in screen time by autism status and gender, contextualizes findings within the evolving digital media landscape, and importantly notes that the association between screen time and obesity did not differ by autism status. The paper acknowledges potential benefits of screen time for children with autism (social interaction, positive reinforcement) and notes that highly prescriptive screen time guidelines have been replaced by more nuanced approaches focusing on content and context.
